task: I need to file a complaint about a purchase that never got resolved, and I’m not sure what to gather before I start. Could you help me figure out the simplest way to put it together and what order to do the steps in?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s a simple, low-stress order you can follow.

## 1) Lay out the basics first
Put these in one pile:
- Receipt or order confirmation
- Account number or order number
- Date of purchase
- Exact amount charged
- Product/service name
- Seller/company name
- Any emails, texts, or chat logs about the problem

## 2) Write a short timeline
In plain language, note:
- What you bought
- When you bought it
- What went wrong
- What you already did to try to fix it
- How the company responded, if they did

## 3) Save proof of the problem
Gather anything that shows the issue:
- Photos or screenshots
- Delivery tracking
- Repair notes
- Return labels
- Call logs with dates and names
- Copies of complaints already sent

## 4) Check for missing key details
Before you send anything, make sure you have:
- Correct company name
- Correct address or complaint form
- Correct account/order number
- Exact amount involved
- Any deadline for filing

## 5) Write the complaint in this order
Use this simple structure:
1. What you bought
2. What went wrong
3. What you already tried
4. What you want now
5. Attach copies of proof

## 6) Keep a copy of everything
Before sending:
- Make a copy or screenshot of the complaint
- Save the date you sent it
- Save the method used: email, mail, online form, or phone

## Quick checklist
- [ ] Receipt/order confirmation
- [ ] Account/order number
- [ ] Exact amount due or charged
- [ ] Timeline of events
- [ ] Proof of the problem
- [ ] Company contact details
- [ ] Copy saved before sending

If you want, I can also help you turn your facts into a short complaint letter or fill-in-the-blank template.

user_simulator.text:
That’s plain enough, and the order makes sense. I’d probably do it just about that way. Start with the receipt, order number, dates, and what was charged. Then write the little timeline in order so it doesn’t get muddled. After that, gather the proof and any copies of messages or call notes.

The only thing I’d add is: write down who you talked to, and the date each time, because that matters later. I learned that the hard way with paperwork once. Keep one folder, paper if you can, and don’t send the original of anything if you can avoid it.

So yes — simple and steady. That works for me.
